constraints.text: Do not follow this OTM chapter or the NEP for explosives and blasting agents.
Do not collect samples of explosives or blasting agents.
29 CFR 1910.132 applies to personal protective equipment (PPE) for dust hazards.

steps.text: Take measurements of dust accumulations.
Select locations and collect a representative sample.
Take well documented photographs of visually excessive overhead accumulations of dust if measurements are not feasible.

preconditions.text: If dust accumulations are obviously less than 5% of the floor area, measurements may not be needed.
When dust is not present throughout the room or building enclosure, the size of each of the dust accumulation areas should be measured.
If the facility has locations with different combustible dusts that could present hazards, consider multiple samples.

steps.text: Ask managers and employees for a copy of the incident reports.
Ask managers and employees for a copy of the OSHA 300 forms and/or other reports.
Ask managers and employees for a copy of the procedures and work practices.
Ask managers and employees for a copy of the procedure(s).
Ask managers and employees for a copy of the documents.
Ask managers and employees for a copy of the hot work procedure.
Ask managers and employees for a copy of the PPE hazard assessment.
Ask managers and engineers for a copy of the bill of materials.
Ask managers and employees for a copy of NFPA 652-2019, section 8.3, and applicable sections of the dust-type NFPA standards.
Ask questions, review AMS design and field installation, identify markings, obtain/review installation documentation, review applicable NFPA standards, and obtain/review manufacturer’s information.
Determine if the AMS contains any recognized methods of active or passive isolation to prevent deflagration propagation out of all AMS openings.
If there is no isolation device on the clean air exhaust outlet, ensure the ducting terminates at a safe, outdoor location away from employees and any air inlets.

Ask questions, review AMS design and field installation, identify markings, obtain/review installation documentation, review applicable NFPA standards, and obtain/review manufacturer’s information regarding exhaust air.
Identify if the AMS has recognized methods of detecting a fire condition and mitigating the hazard.
Determine if the system is being maintained (inspected and tested) per the manufacturer’s recommendations.
Describe any upstream controls in place to detect a fire condition and provide a response.
Safely Collecting Samples Using Approved Equipment and Methods
Do not use this procedure for collecting samples of low-MIE dusts.
Do not use this procedure for collecting samples of metal dusts.
Do not collect samples of combustible dust hybrid mixtures without the approval of your area office.

constraints.text: Wear PPE described in the NEP.
Do not use this procedure for collecting samples of low-MIE dusts.
Do not use this procedure for collecting samples of metal dusts.
Do not collect samples of combustible dust hybrid mixtures without the approval of your area office.

steps.text: Do not collect samples in plastic bags.
Ensure seals on containers are accurate and legible.
Contact the lab if low bulk density dusts require more than one liter.
Make sure OSHA 91A forms are accurate and legible.
Include an SDS with the sample if available.
Keep samples out of the sun.
Store samples in a dry location, clear of other combustible materials, away from sources of heat, away from work areas, and protected from tampering.
Apply the sample seal.
Remove the heel grounders and bonding wires.
Notify the employer that sampling at that location has been completed.
Weigh crucible.
Weigh aliquot of sample in crucible.

constraints.text: Dusts with more than 5% moisture are dried prior to performing explosibility tests.
This test method must be modified when the materials being tested degrade at 75ºC.

steps.text: The sample is dried for 24 hours in a drying oven set at 167o F or 75° C.
The sample is weighed.

constraints.text: A spark or thermal effect is incapable of causing ignition of a flammable or combustible material in air under prescribed test methods.
